question: He said to me, 'I am going to the market. Do you want anything?'
options: He told me that he was going to the market and asked if I wanted anything.
He said to me that he was going to the market and asked did I want anything.
He told me that he is going to the market and asked if I want anything.
He told me that he was going to the market and asked what I wanted.
answer: He told me that he was going to the market and asked if I wanted anything.
explanation: When reporting two sentences, use a connector like 'and' or 'and asked' to join the assertive and interrogative parts. ✅

question: The teacher said to the boy, 'Why are you late? Don't repeat this again.'
options: The teacher asked the boy why he was late and warned him not to repeat that again.
The teacher asked the boy why was he late and told him not to repeat this again.
The teacher questioned the boy's lateness and ordered him to not repeat it.
The teacher told the boy why he was late and advised him to not repeat that.
answer: The teacher asked the boy why he was late and warned him not to repeat that again.
explanation: The first part is a question and the second is a negative command. 'This' changes to 'that'. ✅

question: She said, 'Goodbye! We shall meet again tomorrow.'
options: She bade goodbye and said that they would meet again the next day.
She wished goodbye and said that we shall meet again tomorrow.
She bade goodbye and said that they should meet again the next day.
She said goodbye and told that they will meet again the next day.
answer: She bade goodbye and said that they would meet again the next day.
explanation: 'Goodbye' is reported with 'bade', and 'tomorrow' changes to 'the next day'. ✅

question: Ram said to me, 'I have no money. Can you lend me some?'
options: Ram told me that he had no money and asked if I could lend him some.
Ram told me that he has no money and asked can I lend him some.
Ram told me that he had no money and asked I could lend him some.
Ram said he had no money and requested if I could lend him some.
answer: Ram told me that he had no money and asked if I could lend him some.
explanation: Assertive sentence (had) + Interrogative (if I could). ✅

question: The stranger said to me, 'Where do you live? May I come in?'
options: The stranger asked me where I lived and if he might come in.
The stranger asked me where did I live and might he come in.
The stranger enquired where I lived and asked to come in.
The stranger asked where I live and if he may come in.
answer: The stranger asked me where I lived and if he might come in.
explanation: Double question: 'where I lived' and 'if he might' (May ➔ Might). ✅

question: She said to her mother, 'I am very tired. I want to sleep now.'
options: She told her mother that she was very tired and wanted to sleep then.
She told her mother that she was very tired and that she wants to sleep now.
She told her mother she is tired and wants to sleep then.
She said to her mother that she was tired and she wanted to sleep now.
answer: She told her mother that she was very tired and wanted to sleep then.
explanation: Both are assertive sentences; 'now' changes to 'then'. ✅

question: He said, 'Thank you! You have been very kind.'
options: He thanked me and said that I had been very kind.
He said thank you and told that I was very kind.
He thanked me and said that you had been very kind.
He thanked me and told me that I have been very kind.
answer: He thanked me and said that I had been very kind.
explanation: 'Thank you' is reported as 'thanked [object]'. ✅

question: The captain said to the team, 'Bravo! You played well. Keep it up.'
options: The captain applauded the team, saying they had played well and encouraged them to keep it up.
The captain told the team bravo that they played well and told to keep it up.
The captain exclaimed with joy that they played well and told them to keep it up.
The captain praised the team and said that they had played well and keep it up.
answer: The captain applauded the team, saying they had played well and encouraged them to keep it up.
explanation: Applauded + saying + encouraged (imperative part). ✅

question: I said to him, 'Wait here until I return. Do you understand?'
options: I told him to wait there until I returned and asked if he understood.
I told him to wait here until I returned and asked did he understand.
I ordered him to wait there until I returned and asked if he understands.
I asked him to wait there and asked if he had understood.
answer: I told him to wait there until I returned and asked if he understood.
explanation: Imperative (to wait) + Interrogative (if he understood). ✅

question: He said, 'What a pity! I cannot help you.'
options: He exclaimed with sorrow that it was a great pity and said that he could not help me.
He said what a pity and told that he cannot help me.
He exclaimed that it is a pity and he could not help me.
He told me with sorrow that he can't help me.
answer: He exclaimed with sorrow that it was a great pity and said that he could not help me.
explanation: Exclamatory (great pity) + Assertive (could not). ✅

question: The judge said to the accused, 'Are you guilty? Tell the truth.'
options: The judge asked the accused if he was guilty and ordered him to tell the truth.
The judge asked the accused if he was guilty and told him tell the truth.
The judge asked the accused was he guilty and ordered to tell the truth.
The judge enquired the accused's guilt and asked him to speak truth.
answer: The judge asked the accused if he was guilty and ordered him to tell the truth.
explanation: Interrogative + Imperative. ✅

question: She said to her friend, 'Will you come? I am waiting for you.'
options: She asked her friend if she would come and said that she was waiting for her.
She asked her friend if she will come and said that she is waiting for her.
She asked her friend would she come as she was waiting.
She told her friend that she was waiting and asked her to come.
answer: She asked her friend if she would come and said that she was waiting for her.
explanation: Interrogative (would) + Assertive (was waiting). ✅

question: He said, 'I have lost my pen. Have you seen it anywhere?'
options: He said that he had lost his pen and asked if I had seen it anywhere.
He told that he lost his pen and asked if I have seen it.
He said that he had lost his pen and asked me had I seen it.
He exclaimed that his pen was lost and asked for it.
answer: He said that he had lost his pen and asked if I had seen it anywhere.
explanation: Present Perfect ➔ Past Perfect for both parts. ✅

question: The father said to his son, 'Work hard. Don't waste your time.'
options: The father advised his son to work hard and not to waste his time.
The father told his son to work hard and forbade him to waste time.
The father ordered his son to work hard and not waste time.
Both A and B are correct.
answer: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: Multiple reporting styles for imperative advice/commands are valid. ✅

question: I said to her, 'Happy Birthday! May you have a great year ahead!'
options: I wished her a happy birthday and prayed that she might have a great year ahead.
I wished her happy birthday and said she might have a great year.
I told her happy birthday and wished that she may have a great year.
I greeted her and prayed for her success.
answer: I wished her a happy birthday and prayed that she might have a great year ahead.
explanation: Greeting + Optative sentence. ✅

question: He said to his wife, 'I am leaving for Delhi. I shall be back on Monday.'
options: He told his wife that he was leaving for Delhi and that he would be back on Monday.
He told his wife that he was leaving for Delhi and would be back on Monday.
He told his wife that he is leaving for Delhi and will be back on Monday.
Both A and B are correct.
answer: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: Two assertive sentences can be combined with 'and that' or simply 'and'. ✅

question: The boy said, 'I am sorry. I will never do it again.'
options: The boy apologized and said that he would never do it again.
The boy said he is sorry and that he will never do it again.
The boy told he was sorry and he would never do it again.
The boy exclaimed regretfully that he will never do it again.
answer: The boy apologized and said that he would never do it again.
explanation: 'I am sorry' is best reported as 'apologized'. ✅

question: She said, 'What a beautiful dress! How much did it cost?'
options: She exclaimed that it was a very beautiful dress and asked how much it had cost.
She exclaimed what a beautiful dress it was and asked how much did it cost.
She said it was a beautiful dress and asked its price.
She exclaimed with joy about the dress and asked the cost.
answer: She exclaimed that it was a very beautiful dress and asked how much it had cost.
explanation: Exclamatory + Wh- question (had cost). ✅

question: The master said to the servant, 'Why are you so lazy? Go and finish your work.'
options: The master asked the servant why he was so lazy and ordered him to go and finish his work.
The master asked the servant why was he lazy and told him to finish work.
The master rebuked the servant for being lazy and ordered him to finish work.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: Both A and C are correct.
explanation: 'Asked why' or 'rebuked for' are both excellent ways to report the context. ✅

question: He said to me, 'Congratulations! You have topped the class.'
options: He congratulated me and said that I had topped the class.
He said congratulations and told that I had topped the class.
He congratulated me saying that you have topped the class.
He praised me for topping the class.
answer: He congratulated me and said that I had topped the class.
explanation: 'Congratulations' ➔ 'congratulated [object]'. ✅

question: She said, 'My God! What have I done?'
options: She exclaimed with regret/shock and asked what she had done.
She asked God what she had done.
She exclaimed what she had done.
She told that what have she done.
answer: She exclaimed with regret/shock and asked what she had done.
explanation: 'My God' is an exclamation of shock or regret. ✅

question: The doctor said, 'How is the patient? Has he taken his medicine?'
options: The doctor asked how the patient was and if he had taken his medicine.
The doctor asked how the patient is and if he has taken medicine.
The doctor enquired about the patient and if he had taken medicine.
The doctor asked how was the patient and had he taken medicine.
answer: The doctor asked how the patient was and if he had taken his medicine.
explanation: Two questions: Wh- question and Yes/No question. ✅

question: He said, 'Goodbye, Mother! I am off to school.'
options: He bade his mother goodbye and said that he was off to school.
He said goodbye mother and told he is going to school.
He bade his mother goodbye and said he is off to school.
He told his mother goodbye and he was off to school.
answer: He bade his mother goodbye and said that he was off to school.
explanation: Bade goodbye + assertive part. ✅

question: The boy said to his friend, 'Please lend me your book. I will return it tomorrow.'
options: The boy requested his friend to lend him his book and promised that he would return it the next day.
The boy requested his friend to lend him his book and said he will return it tomorrow.
The boy asked his friend for his book and said he would return it the next day.
The boy requested his friend to lend his book and would return it the next day.
answer: The boy requested his friend to lend him his book and promised that he would return it the next day.
explanation: Request (to lend) + Promise/Statement (would return). ✅

question: I said to her, 'Are you unwell? Can I help you?'
options: I asked her if she was unwell and if I could help her.
I asked her if she is unwell and if I can help her.
I asked her whether she was unwell and can I help you.
I enquired if she was unwell and offered help.
answer: I asked her if she was unwell and if I could help her.
explanation: Two Yes/No questions connected by 'and if'. ✅

question: The old woman said, 'God bless you! May you live long!'
options: The old woman blessed me and prayed that I might live long.
The old woman blessed me and wished that I may live long.
The old woman prayed that God might bless me and live long.
The old woman wished that God blesses me and I live long.
answer: The old woman blessed me and prayed that I might live long.
explanation: Two optative sentences joined. ✅

question: He said, 'Let's go. We are getting late.'
options: He suggested going and said that they were getting late.
He suggested that they should go as they were getting late.
He proposed to go and said we are getting late.
Both A and B are correct.
answer: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: 'Let's' can be reported as a gerund or 'that...should'. ✅

question: She said, 'Welcome! Please come in and have some tea.'
options: She welcomed me and requested me to go in and have some tea.
She welcomed me and asked to come in and have tea.
She said welcome and told me to come in and have tea.
She greeted me and requested me to come in for tea.
answer: She welcomed me and requested me to go in and have some tea.
explanation: 'Welcome' ➔ 'welcomed'. 'Come in' ➔ 'go in'. ✅

question: The father said, 'Well done! You have made me proud.'
options: The father praised him and said that he had made him proud.
The father exclaimed with joy that he had made him proud.
The father told him well done and he made him proud.
The father congratulated him on making him proud.
answer: The father praised him and said that he had made him proud.
explanation: 'Well done' is a phrase of praise/appreciation. ✅

question: He said, 'I'm afraid I can't help you. I'm very busy.'
options: He said that he was afraid he couldn't help me as he was very busy.
He told that he is afraid he can't help me as he is busy.
He expressed his inability to help me as he was busy.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: Both A and C are correct.
explanation: Reporting the speech literally or capturing the sense of 'inability' are both common in advanced exams. ✅
